title: System profile and time baseline: WKS-WIN764BITB, Windows 7 Ultimate SP1 x64, US Eastern (UTC-4 EDT)
observation: fsstat reports a single logical NTFS volume (serial 3624BAAF24BA718B, cluster 4096, no partition table). RegRipper winver on the carved SOFTWARE hive: ProductName 'Windows 7 Ultimate', CSDVersion 'Service Pack 1', BuildLabEx '7601.17727.amd64fre.win7sp1_gdr.111118-2330', InstallDate 2010-11-10 17:10:57Z, RegisteredOwner 'Windows User'. RegRipper compname (SYSTEM): ComputerName = WKS-WIN764BITB. RegRipper timezone (SYSTEM, ControlSet001): TimeZoneKeyName 'Eastern Standard Time', Bias 300 (UTC-5), ActiveTimeBias 240 (UTC-4). RegRipper shutdown (SYSTEM): ShutdownTime 2012-03-15 22:33:26Z. Registry hive $STANDARD_INFORMATION file-modified times (istat) extend to 2012-04-08 (SOFTWARE 03:52:38Z, SYSTEM 01:22:15Z), later than the recorded clean ShutdownTime.
interpretation: Establishes the time baseline for the case: the host is the 64-bit Windows 7 Ultimate SP1 workstation 'WKS-WIN764BITB' configured to US Eastern time. All forensic tool timestamps in this case are UTC (Z); host-local time during the relevant period is EDT (UTC-4). Because hive write activity (to 2012-04-08) postdates the last clean ShutdownTime (2012-03-15), the final disk state was most likely captured by live or forced acquisition rather than after a clean shutdown. The substantive activity window for this triage is approximately 2012-03-13 through 2012-04-08.
confidence_justification: OS build, hostname, timezone, install date and shutdown time are each read from independent registry keys via a validated clean hive carve, and are internally consistent; fsstat independently confirms the NTFS volume. No single-source dependency.

supporting_commands.output_excerpt:
File System Type: NTFS
Volume Serial Number: 3624BAAF24BA718B
Cluster Size: 4096
Total Cluster Range: 0 - 7380990
supporting_commands.purpose: Confirm NTFS logical volume, serial number, cluster size, no partition table

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: ProductName Windows 7 Ultimate / CSDVersion Service Pack 1 / BuildLabEx 7601.17727.amd64fre.win7sp1_gdr.111118-2330 / InstallDate 2010-11-10 17:10:57Z
supporting_commands.purpose: OS product/build/install date

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: ComputerName = WKS-WIN764BITB / TCP/IP Hostname = WKS-WIN764BITB
supporting_commands.purpose: Hostname

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: TimeZoneKeyName-> Eastern Standard Time / Bias 300 (5 hours) / ActiveTimeBias 240 (4 hours)
supporting_commands.purpose: Timezone / bias for time baseline

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: ControlSet001\Control\Windows ShutdownTime : 2012-03-15 22:33:26Z

title: Anomalous locally-created administrator account 'SRL-Helpdesk' (RID 1001) created 2012-03-13
observation: RegRipper samparse on the carved SAM hive lists only three local accounts: Administrator[500], Guest[501, disabled], and SRL-Helpdesk[1001]. SRL-Helpdesk: Account Type 'Default Admin User', Account Created Tue 2012-03-13 23:29:25Z, Last Login 2012-04-05 22:23:21Z, Pwd Reset 2012-04-04 12:41:09Z, Login Count 6. It is a member of the local Administrators group (alongside Administrator[500] and Domain Admins S-1-5-21-2036804247-3058324640-2116585241-512), the Users group, and Remote Desktop Users. Machine SID is S-1-5-21-228041085-1601810819-2769380060, so SRL-Helpdesk SID is ...-1001. ProfileList (SOFTWARE) shows C:\Users\SRL-Helpdesk LastWrite 2012-04-05 22:23:33Z. The original local account Jim (S-1-5-21-228041085-1601810819-2769380060-1000) has a profile directory but no SAM entry.
interpretation: SRL-Helpdesk is the only non-builtin local account on the host, holds local administrator rights and Remote Desktop access, and was created on 2012-03-13 — roughly 16 months after OS install (2010-11-10) — with a name impersonating IT/helpdesk. This is the profile of an attacker-created backdoor local admin account used for persistence and interactive/RDP access (MITRE T1136.001 Create Account: Local Account; T1078.003 Valid Accounts: Local Accounts). Six logons through 2012-04-05 indicate active use during the incident window. The 2012-03-13 creation is a candidate initial-foothold marker. Alternative benign explanation: a legitimately provisioned helpdesk support account; this should be corroborated against Security event log account-management events (4720 create, 4724/4723 password, 4732 add-to-Administrators) and logon events before concluding malice.
confidence_justification: The SAM creation timestamp, admin group membership and RID are definitive primary-source facts (single hive, internally consistent). Confidence held at MEDIUM rather than HIGH because the malicious interpretation is not yet corroborated by a second independent source (event logs / logon artifacts), which is the next step.

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: Username : SRL-Helpdesk [1001] / Account Type : Default Admin User / Account Created : Tue Mar 13 23:29:25 2012 Z / Last Login Date : Thu Apr 5 22:23:21 2012 Z / Login Count : 6 ... Group Name : Administrators [3] ... S-1-5-21-228041085-1601810819-2769380060-1001
supporting_commands.purpose: Enumerate local SAM accounts, creation/login timestamps, and group membership
Confirm SRL-Helpdesk profile and distinguish local vs domain SIDs

title: Planted malware C:\Windows\System32\spinlock.exe (PyInstaller/Python, timestomped) deployed 2012-04-05
observation: fls/grep located Windows/System32/spinlock.exe (MFT 540). istat 540: $DATA size 2,271,885 bytes; $STANDARD_INFORMATION Created 2012-04-05 16:54:26Z, Modified 2012-04-04 17:06:37Z, Accessed 2012-04-05 16:54:26Z; $FILE_NAME Created/Modified/Accessed all 2012-04-05 16:54:26Z; Security ID 749 = S-1-5-32-544 (Administrators). The $SI Modified time (2012-04-04 17:06:37) is earlier than both the $SI Created and the $FN Created (2012-04-05 16:54:26). A byte-exact dd carve yields: file = PE32 executable (GUI) Intel 80386, 4 sections; SHA-256 6eef2381040cd38ce5974ef954121e136bd93ec4039d49925438c92ef5f3dead; MD5 6bff2aebb8852fc2658b9768d2166ece. ASCII strings include _MEIPASS2, bpython25.dll, %spython%02d.dll, PYTHONHOME=, PYTHONPATH=, 'Error loading Python DLL' — PyInstaller-bundled Python 2.5.
interpretation: spinlock.exe is an attacker-deployed executable: a non-Windows PyInstaller-packed Python 2.5 program placed directly in System32 during the incident window (2012-04-05). Its location masquerades as a system component (MITRE T1036.005), and the $SI Modified timestamp preceding the $FN Created time is a timestomping indicator (T1070.006). It is a Python-based tool/backdoor (T1059.006) and is the payload launched by scheduled task At1 (cmd /c c:\windows\system32\spinlock.exe). Network/C2 indicators are not present in plaintext strings because the Python payload is compressed inside the PyInstaller archive — recovering them would require unpacking (evidence gap). Alternative benign explanation (a legitimately named admin utility) is refuted by the non-standard System32 placement, timestomping, and AT-job execution.
confidence_justification: The binary is present and hashed; PE32 + PyInstaller/Python confirmed by file and strings; planting and timestomping are established from independent $SI vs $FN MFT timestamps; execution is corroborated by an independent artifact (At1 task XML). Multiple independent sources.

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: Name: spinlock.exe / $SI Created 2012-04-05 16:54:26 / $SI Modified 2012-04-04 17:06:37 / $FN Created 2012-04-05 16:54:26 / $DATA size 2271885 / Security ID 749 (S-1-5-32-544)
supporting_commands.purpose: spinlock.exe MAC times, size, owner SID, timestomping evidence

supporting_commands.command: dd if=ewf1 bs=1048576 iflag=skip_bytes,count_bytes skip=22842863616 count=2271885 of=spinlock.exe.bin ; sha256sum ; md5sum ; file
supporting_commands.output_excerpt: 2271885 bytes copied; SHA-256 6eef2381040cd38ce5974ef954121e136bd93ec4039d49925438c92ef5f3dead; MD5 6bff2aebb8852fc2658b9768d2166ece; PE32 executable (GUI) Intel 80386, 4 sections
supporting_commands.purpose: Byte-exact carve, hash, and type-confirm spinlock.exe

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: _MEIPASS2 / bpython25.dll / %spython%02d.dll / PYTHONHOME= / PYTHONPATH= / Error loading Python DLL: %s (error code %d)
supporting_commands.purpose: Identify PyInstaller/Python packing

title: Persistence + execution: malicious HKLM Run key and two AT scheduled tasks (At1/At2) created 2012-04-05
observation: RegRipper run plugin on SOFTWARE.bin shows Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run (LastWrite 2012-04-05 17:53:13Z) containing value 'svchost' = 'c:\windows\system32\dllhost\svchost.exe' alongside legitimate entries (Adobe ARM, McAfee, Adobe Reader SL). icat of System32\Tasks\At1 (MFT 15623) is a Task XML: TimeTrigger StartBoundary 2012-04-05T13:01:00, Principal UserId @AtServiceAccount, RunLevel HighestAvailable, Action Exec Command 'cmd' Arguments '/c c:\windows\system32\spinlock.exe'. icat of System32\Tasks\At2 (MFT 92254): StartBoundary 2012-04-05T13:47:00, same principal, Action 'cmd' '/c c:\windows\system32\dllhost\svchost.exe'.
interpretation: Three persistence/execution mechanisms created on 2012-04-05 converge on two attacker payloads. The Run key value 'svchost' pointing to c:\windows\system32\dllhost\svchost.exe is autostart persistence (MITRE T1547.001) using a binary masqueraded as svchost inside a non-standard 'dllhost' subfolder (T1036.005). At1 and At2 are 'at'-command scheduled tasks (T1053.002) that execute spinlock.exe and dllhost\svchost.exe respectively. Together they establish reboot-surviving persistence and on-demand execution of the malware identified in F-jgore-003. Benign alternative (legitimate helpdesk/admin automation) is refuted by the masqueraded svchost path, the generic At1/At2 naming, and the link to the planted spinlock.exe.
confidence_justification: Three independent artifacts of two different types (registry Run key + two scheduled-task XML files) corroborate each other and tie to the separately-confirmed malware spinlock.exe (F-003). Paths and timestamps are read verbatim from primary sources.

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run LastWrite 2012-04-05 17:53:13Z : svchost - c:\windows\system32\dllhost\svchost.exe
supporting_commands.purpose: Enumerate HKLM Run/RunOnce persistence

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: <StartBoundary>2012-04-05T13:01:00</StartBoundary> ... <UserId>@AtServiceAccount</UserId> <RunLevel>HighestAvailable</RunLevel> <Command>cmd</Command><Arguments>/c c:\windows\system32\spinlock.exe</Arguments>
supporting_commands.purpose: Read At1 scheduled task definition

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: <StartBoundary>2012-04-05T13:47:00</StartBoundary> ... <Command>cmd</Command><Arguments>/c c:\windows\system32\dllhost\svchost.exe</Arguments>
supporting_commands.purpose: Read At2 scheduled task definition

title: Exclusion: System32 svchost.exe and dllhost.exe are legitimate; masqueraded dllhost\svchost.exe payload is absent (deleted) — evidence gap
observation: istat of Windows/System32/svchost.exe (MFT 25174): $SI Created 2009-07-13 23:31:13Z, $FN Created 2010-11-10 18:47:44Z, owner SID S-1-5-80-... (TrustedInstaller), $DATA 27,136 bytes. istat of Windows/System32/dllhost.exe (MFT 23430): $SI Created 2009-07-13 23:59:17Z, $FN Created 2010-11-10 18:46:27Z, TrustedInstaller, $DATA 9,728 bytes. fls of inode 23430 returns 'no $IDX_ROOT' (it is a file, not a directory). grep of the recursive fls listing for 'System32/dllhost/' (directory) returns no results; no spinlock-style payload exists at c:\windows\system32\dllhost\svchost.exe in the live filesystem.
interpretation: The System32 svchost.exe and dllhost.exe present on disk are the genuine Windows binaries (Win7 build creation 2009-07-13, install-time $FN 2010-11-10, TrustedInstaller-owned, expected sizes) and are excluded as benign — they are NOT the malware. The malicious 'svchost' referenced by the Run key and task At2 is the distinct path c:\windows\system32\dllhost\svchost.exe (a svchost placed inside a non-standard 'dllhost' folder), which does not exist in the allocated filesystem at acquisition time. This is an evidence gap: the payload binary was deleted or never persisted to disk in the captured state, so it could not be hashed; the persistence/task references (F-jgore-004) nonetheless prove it existed and was scheduled to run. Recovery could be attempted from unallocated space / USN journal (not yet performed).
confidence_justification: Legitimacy of svchost.exe/dllhost.exe is established by independent $SI/$FN timestamps, TrustedInstaller ownership and original sizes. Absence of the dllhost\svchost.exe payload is confirmed by both targeted fls (no directory) and grep over the full recursive listing. Stated as exclusion + explicit gap per framework guidance.

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: Name: svchost.exe / $SI Created 2009-07-13 23:31:13 / $FN Created 2010-11-10 18:47:44 / Security ID 451 (S-1-5-80 TrustedInstaller) / $DATA size 27136
supporting_commands.purpose: Confirm System32 svchost.exe is the legitimate Windows binary

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: Name: dllhost.exe / $SI Created 2009-07-13 23:59:17 / $FN Created 2010-11-10 18:46:27 / Security ID 451 (TrustedInstaller) / $DATA size 9728
supporting_commands.purpose: Confirm System32 dllhost.exe is the legitimate Windows binary

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: fls 23430: 'Attribute 144 not found ... $IDX_ROOT not found' (dllhost.exe is a file); grep for System32/dllhost/ directory: no matches
supporting_commands.purpose: Confirm no dllhost directory / no dllhost\svchost.exe payload present

title: SRL-Helpdesk backdoor account interactive use: Computer Management, Remote Desktop, cmd (UserAssist ~2012-04-04)
observation: RegRipper userassist on the reconstructed SRL-Helpdesk NTUSER.DAT (dd-carved from MFT 90723, fragmented runs 1204252-1204379 + 3063109-3063119) shows UserAssist key LastWrite 2012-04-04 05:41:21Z. GUI execution entries (GUID {CEBFF5CD-...}) include: CompMgmtLauncher.exe (2012-04-04 20:00:47Z), and under 2012-04-04 05:39:34Z: calc.exe, SnippingTool.exe, mspaint.exe, magnify.exe, solitaire.exe, Microsoft.Windows.RemoteDesktop, Microsoft.Windows.GettingStarted; value names without timestamps include cmd.exe and SystemPropertiesProtection.exe. The NTUSER.DAT $SI Created 2012-03-15 21:20:10Z (profile first created), File Modified 2012-04-06 13:43:16Z.
interpretation: The SRL-Helpdesk local admin account (F-jgore-002) was used interactively (hands-on-keyboard) on 2012-04-04: it launched Computer Management (CompMgmtLauncher.exe — the console used to create/modify local users and group memberships), the Remote Desktop client, and cmd.exe. This corroborates that SRL-Helpdesk is an actively-operated account rather than a dormant provisioned one, and is consistent with an attacker using it for account management and lateral movement over RDP (MITRE T1078.003, T1021.001). The profile creation (2012-03-15) and SAM account creation (2012-03-13, F-002) are close in time. Alternative benign explanation (a real helpdesk technician performing support) remains possible but is weakened by the account's anomalous creation and the surrounding malware/persistence findings.
confidence_justification: UserAssist entries are primary-source facts from a validated hive reconstruction and independently corroborate the SAM-derived account finding (F-002), raising overall confidence in active use. Held at MEDIUM (not HIGH) for the malicious-intent inference, which still lacks Security event-log logon-type corroboration (parser unavailable).

supporting_commands.command: dd ...skip=1204252 count=128 ; dd ...skip=3063109 count=11 oflag=append ; rip.pl -r NTUSER_SRL.bin -p userassist
supporting_commands.output_excerpt: UserAssist LastWrite 2012-04-04 05:41:21Z; {CEBFF5CD...} 2012-04-04 20:00:47Z CompMgmtLauncher.exe (1); Microsoft.Windows.RemoteDesktop (8); cmd.exe (no timestamp)
supporting_commands.purpose: Reconstruct SRL-Helpdesk NTUSER.DAT and parse UserAssist GUI execution

title: USB mass-storage device (HP v100w) connected 2012-04-07, day before acquisition
observation: RegRipper usbstor on SYSTEM.bin: ControlSet001\Enum\USBStor contains a single device Disk&Ven_HP&Prod_v100w&Rev_1024, S/N AA951D0000007252&0, FriendlyName 'HP v100w USB Device', with first-insert and all key LastWrite timestamps 2012-04-07 18:50:55Z. Image acquisition occurred ~2012-04-08 (hive file-modified times).
interpretation: Exactly one USB mass-storage device has ever been enumerated on this host, an HP v100w flash drive connected 2012-04-07 18:50:55Z — less than a day before acquisition. This could be (a) responder/acquisition media used during collection, or (b) attacker-staging/exfiltration media. The single timestamp does not by itself indicate data transfer. Drive-letter/volume correlation (MountedDevices / MountPoints2) and file-access artifacts would be needed to determine usage; not yet performed. Recorded as a neutral observation pending that correlation.
confidence_justification: The device record and timestamp are definitive from USBSTOR, but the purpose (benign responder media vs malicious staging) is undetermined without further correlation; confidence reflects interpretation uncertainty, not the fact of connection.

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: Disk&Ven_HP&Prod_v100w&Rev_1024 [2012-04-07 18:50:55] S/N: AA951D0000007252&0 FriendlyName: HP v100w USB Device
supporting_commands.purpose: Enumerate USB storage device history

title: IOC sweep: custom YARA rule detects spinlock.exe; anti-forensics (BCWipe) present; chrome_updater.exe initial-access lead
observation: A custom YARA rule (SRL_spinlock_pyinstaller) authored at /cases/e2e-demo/extractions/rules/spinlock_iocs.yar matched the carved spinlock.exe.bin, with string hits: $mz 4D5A at 0x0, $mei _MEIPASS2 at 0x14400/0x14468, $py bpython25.dll at 0x22aa16, $pyver %spython%02d.dll at 0x14e3c, $pyhome PYTHONHOME= at 0x14f10, $err 'Error loading Python DLL' at 0x14e0c. AppCompatCache (ShimCache, SYSTEM.bin) has LastWrite 2012-03-15 22:33:26Z (equal to last clean ShutdownTime); spinlock.exe is absent from ShimCache (cache not flushed after the 2012-03-15 shutdown, so the 2012-04 malware activity post-dates it). ShimCache contains the Jetico BCWipe suite executed: C:\Program Files (x86)\Jetico\BCWipe\{BCWipe.exe, BCWipeSvc.exe, BCResident.exe, BCWipeTM.exe, bcupdt.exe} and an install via C:\Users\rsydow\AppData\Local\Temp\~bcwipe5.TMP\Setup.exe. ShimCache also shows repeated C:\Users\nfury\AppData\Local\Google\Update\Install\{GUID}\chrome_updater.exe executions (2011-2012).
interpretation: The custom YARA rule provides a reusable detection signature for the spinlock.exe PyInstaller backdoor (F-jgore-003) and confirms its PyInstaller/Python characteristics by content. The ShimCache LastWrite equalling the last clean shutdown explains why spinlock.exe is not listed there — a temporal artifact, not exculpatory. The presence and execution of Jetico BCWipe (secure-deletion / anti-forensics software, MITRE T1070.004 / T1485) provides a plausible mechanism for the deleted dllhost\svchost.exe payload (F-005) and wiped Prefetch entries, though the recorded BCWipe exec timestamps are 2011 and do not themselves prove wiping during the incident. chrome_updater.exe running from nfury's per-user Google Update GUID folders is suspicious because Google's genuine updater is GoogleUpdate.exe, not chrome_updater.exe; this is a candidate initial-access/masquerading artifact (T1036) in the primary user's profile that warrants deeper analysis (carve + hash + YARA), not yet performed.
confidence_justification: The YARA match and ShimCache entries are deterministic primary-source facts. BCWipe-as-anti-forensics and chrome_updater.exe-as-malicious are interpretive leads with circumstantial support (naming, location, timing) and are explicitly flagged for follow-up; hence MEDIUM overall.

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: SRL_spinlock_pyinstaller spinlock.exe.bin; 0x14400:$mei: _MEIPASS2; 0x22aa16:$py: bpython25.dll; SRL_spinlock_known_hash (filesize==2271885)
supporting_commands.purpose: Detection signature for the PyInstaller backdoor

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: ShimCache LastWrite 2012-03-15 22:33:26Z; C:\Program Files (x86)\Jetico\BCWipe\BCWipe.exe Executed; C:\Users\nfury\AppData\Local\Google\Update\Install\{GUID}\chrome_updater.exe Executed; (no spinlock.exe entry)
supporting_commands.purpose: Anti-forensics tooling and initial-access lead from ShimCache

title: Evidence/tooling gaps: Windows event logs unparsed (no EVTX parser); Security.evtx small; C2 packed
observation: Security.evtx (MFT 57679), System.evtx (57677) and Application.evtx (57678) are present in Windows\System32\winevt\Logs. No EVTX parser is installed in this environment (EvtxECmd, plaso/log2timeline, and hayabusa all report unavailable; only RegRipper/SleuthKit/yara/strings/Volatility3 are present). Security.evtx istat shows $DATA logical size 1,118,208 bytes but initialized size only 80,896 bytes (most of the file is sparse/zero), and is fragmented (runs 173571-173587 + 5338408-5338410). spinlock.exe (F-003) is PyInstaller-packed, so its Python payload and any C2 indicators are zlib-compressed and not recoverable via strings without unpacking.
interpretation: Three evidence gaps are recorded explicitly (absence of evidence is not evidence of absence): (1) Windows Security/System/Application event logs could not be parsed for account-management (4720/4722/4732), logon (4624/4625, RDP type 10) or log-clearing (1102) events because no EVTX parser exists in this toolset — a tooling limitation; these events would be the primary corroboration for SRL-Helpdesk creation/logons (F-002/F-006) and should be parsed on a workstation with EvtxECmd/plaso. (2) The small initialized size of Security.evtx (~80 KB) is consistent with either a low-activity workstation or log truncation/clearing and warrants review once parseable. (3) spinlock.exe network/C2 indicators require PyInstaller unpacking (e.g., on REMnux) to recover. None of these gaps contradict the staged findings; they bound what remains unverified.
confidence_justification: The presence of the event-log files and the absence of a parser are directly observed (fls + check_tools), as is the Security.evtx initialized-size from istat. This finding states verified facts about gaps, not an interpretive conclusion.

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: Security.evtx (57679), System.evtx (57677), Application.evtx (57678) present; EvtxECmd/log2timeline/hayabusa: available=false
supporting_commands.purpose: Confirm event logs present but no parser available

supporting_commands.output_excerpt: $DATA size 1118208 init_size 80896; runs 173571-173587 + 5338408-5338410 then sparse
supporting_commands.purpose: Security.evtx size/initialized-size/fragmentation
